Title Optional python site-packages path in repodata
Status Proposed
Author(s) Jonathan Helmus <jjhelmus@gmail.com>
Created Sep 13, 2024
Updated Sep 13, 2024
Discussion https://github.com/conda/ceps/pull/90
Implementation NA
+ +To avoid confusion this document will use a stylized `python` to indicate a conda package with that name. +Other uses of “Python” will be followed by a qualifier, like "programming language" or "interpreter". +Specific implementations of the Python programming language will be referred to by name, such as CPython or PyPy. + +## Abstract + +We propose adding a new optional field in repodata.json that `python` packages can use to specify the destination path when installing `noarch: python` packages. + +## Background + +In the conda ecosystem the package named `python` provides, either directly or via a dependency, an interpreter for the Python programming language. +This interpreter is often CPython but can also be alternative implementations such as PyPy or GraalPy. + +Tools like conda, mamba and pixi support installing `noarch: python` packages into conda environments. +Whereas the content of other conda packages are linked into the target environment based upon their path within the package, files within the "site-packages" directory of `noarch: python` packages are linked into the site-packages directory of the target environment. + +In conda 24.7.1 and many other versions the location of the site-packages directory within the environment is "Lib/site-packages" on Windows and "lib/pythonX.Y/site-packages" on other platforms where X and Y and the major and minor versions of the `python` package installed in the environment. +These paths are the defaults for CPython but are not the correct paths for alternative implementation of the Python programming language or for some configurations of CPython. +For example PyPy uses "lib/pypyX.Y/site-packages" on POSIX systems. + +In these cases kludgy workarounds, [often a symlink](https://github.com/conda-forge/pypy3.6-feedstock/blob/ac4201e80432469971626d277718edf80b22d6ef/recipe/build.sh#L102), are used in the `python` package to support the installation of `noarch: python` packages. +In the next section a new field will be proposed that allows `python` package to explicitly declare the location of the site-packages directory to avoid the need for these work-arounds. + +## Specification + +An optional field `python_site_packages_path` may be included in a conda package’s info/index.json file as a string and in the corresponding repodata.json entry for the package. +When defined, this field specifies the path of the Python interpreter’s site-packages directory relative to the root of the environment. +If a `python` package that includes this field is installed into an environment, all `noarch: python` packages that are installed into the environment will have the files in their "site-packages" directory linked into the path specified by this field. + +A value of "null" can be used to indicate that this field is not specified. +It is acceptable to not include the field in repodata.json in this case. + +If this field is missing or specified as "null" a default site-packages path will be used. +This path is "Lib/site-packages" if the environment targets a Windows platform and "lib/pythonX.Y/site-packages" on other platforms where X and Y and the major and minor versions of the `python` package installed in the environment. +This matches the current behavior of conda, mamba and pixi. + +This field should only be included in the metadata for `python` packages but tools must not fail if packages with other names include this field, rather the entry should be ignored. + +To avoid unnecessarily increasing the size of repodata.json, it is highly recommended that this field only be included in `python` packages where it is required, that is where the site-packages directory is not the default value. +Packages with other names or `python` packages that use the default site-packages path should not include this field. + +This path must not point to a location outside of the root of the environment (e.g. it cannot navigate up a directory or be an absolute path). +If a package specifies a path which violates this requirement the package must not be installed and an appropriate error should be shown to the user. + +A possible way to check the validity of the `python_site_packages_path` field is with this function: + +``` Python +def is_valid(target_prefix: str, python_site_packages_path: str) -> bool: + target_prefix = os.path.realpath(target_prefix) + full_path = os.path.realpath(os.path.join(target_prefix, python_site_packages_path)) + test_prefix = os.path.commonpath((target_prefix, full_path)) + return test_prefix == target_prefix +``` + +When a package which includes this optional field is indexed the `python_site_packages_path` field will be included in the repodata entry for the package. +For example the repodata entry for a python-3.13.0rc1 package using the free-threading build configuration might look as follows: + +``` + "python-3.13.0rc1-haa6bb3f_0_cpython_cp313t.tar.bz2": { + "build": "haa6bb3f_0_cpython_cp313t", + "build_number": 0, + "depends": [ + "bzip2 >=1.0.8,<2.0a0", + "expat >=2.6.2,<3.0a0", + "libffi >=3.4,<4.0a0", + "ncurses >=6.4,<7.0a0", + "openssl >=3.0.14,<4.0a0", + "readline >=8.1.2,<9.0a0", + "sqlite >=3.45.3,<4.0a0", + "tk >=8.6.14,<8.7.0a0", + "tzdata", + "xz >=5.4.6,<6.0a0", + "zlib >=1.2.13,<1.3.0a0" + ], + "license": "PSF-2.0", + "license_family": "PSF", + "timestamp": 1722610680768, + "track_features": "free-threading", + "md5": "c09289eb86239e1221533457d861f1a3", + "name": "python", + "size": 16332720, + "subdir": osx-arm64, + "version": "3.13.0rc1", + "sha256": "fa0ae22c13450fe6c30c754ee5efbd7fe7e7533b878d7be96e74de56211d19df", + "python_site_packages_path": Description This CEP proposed to introduce an optional `python_site_packages_path` field in repodata that `python` packages can use to explicitly specify the location of the site-packages directory. This allows for `noarch: python` packages to be linked into the correct location when this path does not match the on used by standard builds of CPython. This includes alternative implementation of the Python programming langauge (PyPy, GraalPy) and free-threading builds of CPython. The motivation and request for this CEP came from conda/conda#14053, which discusses `noarch: python` support in conda in environments with the experimental free-threading configuration of CPython This is a draft implementation of the CEP proposal in https://github.com/conda/ceps/pull/90

The location of the site-packages directory can be specified in `python_site_packages_path` in the build section of a recipe. To avoid unnecessarily increasing the size of repodata this metadata should only be specified when the package name is `python` and when the path is not the default for CPython. These suggestions are **not** checked or enforced by conda-build.

Description

This is a draft implementation of the CEP proposal in https://github.com/conda/ceps/pull/90

The location of the site-packages directory can be specified in `python_site_packages_path` in the build section of a recipe. To avoid unnecessarily increasing the size of repodata this metadata should only be specified when the package name is `python` and when the path is not the default for CPython. These suggestions are **not** checked or enforced by conda-build.

Description

This CEP proposed to introduce an optional `python_site_packages_path` field in repodata that `python` packages can use to explicitly specify the location of the site-packages directory. This allows for `noarch: python` packages to be linked into the correct location when this path does not match the on used by standard builds of CPython. This includes alternative implementation of the Python programming langauge (PyPy, GraalPy) and free-threading builds of CPython.

The motivation and request for this CEP came from conda/conda#14053, which discusses `noarch: python` support in conda in environments with the experimental free-threading configuration of CPython 3.13.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/ceps/issues/90/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":1,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/ceps/issues/90/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/ceps/issues/comments/2364513690","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/ceps/pull/90#issuecomment-2364513690","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/ceps/issues/90","id":2364513690,"node_id":"IC_kwDOFruETM6M752a","user":{"login":"jjhelmus","id":1050278,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTAyNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1050278?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus","html_url":"https://github.com/jjhelmus","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-20T19:47:06Z","updated_at":"2024-09-20T19:47:06Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I have put together a draft PR for conda-build that implements support for specifying this field in the build section of a recipe, see conda/conda-build#5502.

There are three noarch packages with the following paths specified
* python 3.99.99 : `lib/python3.99t/site-packages` - A "free-threading" like path

More work is needed for a complete implementation, especially given that this one breaks backwards compatibility in various function.

### Description

When the `python` package in an environment specifies a `python_site_packages_path` in its repodata entry, use that path to install `noarch: python` packages.  

Note that this currently only works with the classic solver as libmamba/conda-libmamba-solver does not yet understand this field.

The current recipe is in the [`py313rc1` branch of the AnacondaRecipes fork](https://github.com/AnacondaRecipes/python-feedstock/tree/py313rc1). Packages are in the `ad-testing/label/py313` channel or anaconda.org. Python 3.13 has an [experimental free-threading option](https://docs.python.org/3.13/whatsnew/3.13.html#free-threaded-cpython) that changes the ABI and platform tag (cp313t vs cp313) as well as the directory where 3rd party packags (e.g. site-packages) resides on POSIX (linux, macOS) platforms. The recipe above creates builds both with the GIL and with the experimental free-threading option. It would be great to align on how to approach these changes.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/python-feedstock/issues/comments/2269502509/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

To use the bundled copy use `--with-system-libmpdec=no`. Longer term a libmpdec conda package should be created and linked. * The python library directory for the free-threading variant includes a `t` suffix, `lib/python3.13t`. In general this suffix is added in various locations (the shared library, directories, etc). One option it to include the `t` in `$VERABI` in the free-threading variant and replace various instances of `$VER` with `$VERABI` ``` if [[ ${PY_FREETHREADING} == true ]]; then THREAD=t else THREAD= fi VERABI=${VER}${DBG}${THREAD} ... ``` * Because of the above, the site-packages directory is in the wrong location for `noarch: python` packages, resulting in them not being importable after install (see conda/conda#14053). A symlink to the location without a suffix in the free-threading variant is a work around: ``` if [[ ${PY_FREETHREADING} == true ]]; then ln -s "${PREFIX}/lib/python${VERABI}" "${PREFIX}/lib/python${VER}" fi ```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/python-feedstock/issues/comments/2276755361/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open reports and couldn't find a duplicate

### What happened?

Installing `noarch: python` packages in an environment with a free-threading version of Python 3.13 results in the package contents not being available for import. 

```
❯ conda create -n bug -c ad-testing/label/py313_nogil python=3.13.0b4 pip --yes
Channels:
 - ad-testing/label/py313_nogil
 - defaults
Platform: osx-arm64
Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: /Users/jhelmus/mc/envs/bug

  added / updated specs:
    - pip
    - python=3.13.0b4


The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

  bzip2              pkgs/main/osx-arm64::bzip2-1.0.8-h80987f9_6
  ca-certificates    pkgs/main/osx-arm64::ca-certificates-2024.7.2-hca03da5_0
  expat              pkgs/main/osx-arm64::expat-2.6.2-h313beb8_0
  libcxx             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::libcxx-14.0.6-h848a8c0_0
  libffi             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::libffi-3.4.4-hca03da5_1
  ncurses            pkgs/main/osx-arm64::ncurses-6.4-h313beb8_0
  openssl            pkgs/main/osx-arm64::openssl-3.0.14-h80987f9_0
  pip                ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::pip-24.1.2-py313_0
  python             ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::python-3.13.0b4-h99e199e_nogil_0
  python_abi         ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::python_abi-3.13-4_cp313t
  readline           pkgs/main/osx-arm64::readline-8.2-h1a28f6b_0
  sqlite             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::sqlite-3.45.3-h80987f9_0
  tk                 pkgs/main/osx-arm64::tk-8.6.14-h6ba3021_0
  tzdata             pkgs/main/noarch::tzdata-2024a-h04d1e81_0
  xz                 pkgs/main/osx-arm64::xz-5.4.6-h80987f9_1
  zlib               pkgs/main/osx-arm64::zlib-1.2.13-h18a0788_1



Downloading and Extracting Packages:

Preparing transaction: done
Verifying transaction: done
Executing transaction: done
#
# To activate this environment, use
#
#     $ conda activate bug
#
# To deactivate an active environment, use
#
#     $ conda deactivate


❯ conda activate bug
❯ python --version
Python 3.13.0b4
❯ python -c "import pip; print(pip.__version__)"
24.1.2

❯ conda install -c defaults -c conda-forge imagesize --yes
Channels:
 - defaults
 - conda-forge
 - ad-testing/label/py313_nogil
Platform: osx-arm64
Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: /Users/jhelmus/mc/envs/bug

  added / updated specs:
    - imagesize


The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

  imagesize          pkgs/main/noarch::imagesize-1.3.0-pyhd3eb1b0_0



Downloading and Extracting Packages:

Preparing transaction: done
Verifying transaction: done
Executing transaction: done

❯ python -c "import imagesize"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
    import imagesize
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'imagesize'

```

The issue here is that the `noarch: python` package is installed into `<PREFIX>/lib/python3.13/site-packages` but the free-threading libraries are expected in `<PREFIX>/lib/python3.13t`, see python/cpython#121103:

```
❯ ls ~/mc/envs/bug/lib/python3.13t/site-packages
README.txt           pip                  pip-24.1.2.dist-info

❯ ls ~/mc/envs/bug/lib/python3.13/site-packages
__pycache__               imagesize-1.3.0.dist-info imagesize.py
```

I assumed this would also be incorrect for the free-threading version of Python 3.13 but a quick test showed that the logic used correctly returns the path with python3.13t.  

The logic that conda-build uses is a glob pattern to find paths matching  `lib/python?.*` in the prefix, removing any symlinks, and return the first result, https://github.com/conda/conda-build/blob/24.7.1/conda_build/utils.py#L971-L982.

Would this be a reasonable approach for conda to adopt?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/comments/2278864800/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

think these paths could also be added to sys.path using [`.pth` files](https://docs.python.org/3/library/site.html) which would leave `sitecustomize.py` available for other uses and would not break if `sitecustomize.py` was clobbered or deleted. [Pex](https://pypi.org/project/pex/) loads modules from multiple directories by adding entries to sys.path. I've had experience with pex and most projects work fine with this setup. There are a few cases where the setup can fail where the packages work fine with a single site-packages directory: * [Namespace packages](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/guides/packaging-namespace-packages/) that use different namespace conventions. * Packages that load data, config or other files from other packages by navigating the filesystem. For example loading a config file using `os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(mod.__file__)), "mod_data", "config.yaml")`. This type of access really should be using `importlib.resources`. Personally, I see benefits and downsides to this approach. I can imagine there may be confusion to users and developers if they see multiple site-packages directories which contain files. Which one is the correct one? All of these directories need to be examined to determine which projects are available to Python. Using symlinks clarifies this some but leaves multiple directories. On the other hand if conda/mamba/pixie/pip places files in the correct site-packages directory then the workaround directories do not exist with this appoarch. There is no confusion or uncertainty. With the symlink approach the symlinks are always there. One other gotya is that conda-build will set $SP_DIR to the first directory matching `lib/python?.*` that is not a symlink, https://github.com/conda/conda-build/blob/24.7.1/conda_build/utils.py#L971-L982. With this approach a noarch: python package installed into a free-threading environment will result in the directory being set incorrectly to the the `python3.13` directory. The logic that conda-build uses to set `SP_DIR` can be made more robust to avoid Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open reports and couldn't find a duplicate

### What happened?

Installing `noarch: python` packages in an environment with a free-threading version of Python 3.13 results in the package contents not being available for import. 

```
❯ conda create -n bug -c ad-testing/label/py313_nogil python=3.13.0b4 pip --yes
Channels:
 - ad-testing/label/py313_nogil
 - defaults
Platform: osx-arm64
Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: /Users/jhelmus/mc/envs/bug

  added / updated specs:
    - pip
    - python=3.13.0b4


The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

  bzip2              pkgs/main/osx-arm64::bzip2-1.0.8-h80987f9_6
  ca-certificates    pkgs/main/osx-arm64::ca-certificates-2024.7.2-hca03da5_0
  expat              pkgs/main/osx-arm64::expat-2.6.2-h313beb8_0
  libcxx             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::libcxx-14.0.6-h848a8c0_0
  libffi             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::libffi-3.4.4-hca03da5_1
  ncurses            pkgs/main/osx-arm64::ncurses-6.4-h313beb8_0
  openssl            pkgs/main/osx-arm64::openssl-3.0.14-h80987f9_0
  pip                ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::pip-24.1.2-py313_0
  python             ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::python-3.13.0b4-h99e199e_nogil_0
  python_abi         ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::python_abi-3.13-4_cp313t
  readline           pkgs/main/osx-arm64::readline-8.2-h1a28f6b_0
  sqlite             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::sqlite-3.45.3-h80987f9_0
  tk                 pkgs/main/osx-arm64::tk-8.6.14-h6ba3021_0
  tzdata             pkgs/main/noarch::tzdata-2024a-h04d1e81_0
  xz                 pkgs/main/osx-arm64::xz-5.4.6-h80987f9_1
  zlib               pkgs/main/osx-arm64::zlib-1.2.13-h18a0788_1



Downloading and Extracting Packages:

Preparing transaction: done
Verifying transaction: done
Executing transaction: done
#
# To activate this environment, use
#
#     $ conda activate bug
#
# To deactivate an active environment, use
#
#     $ conda deactivate


❯ conda activate bug
❯ python --version
Python 3.13.0b4
❯ python -c "import pip; print(pip.__version__)"
24.1.2

❯ conda install -c defaults -c conda-forge imagesize --yes
Channels:
 - defaults
 - conda-forge
 - ad-testing/label/py313_nogil
Platform: osx-arm64
Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: /Users/jhelmus/mc/envs/bug

  added / updated specs:
    - imagesize


The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

  imagesize          pkgs/main/noarch::imagesize-1.3.0-pyhd3eb1b0_0



Downloading and Extracting Packages:

Preparing transaction: done
Verifying transaction: done
Executing transaction: done

❯ python -c "import imagesize"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
    import imagesize
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'imagesize'

```

The issue here is that the `noarch: python` package is installed into `<PREFIX>/lib/python3.13/site-packages` but the free-threading libraries are expected in `<PREFIX>/lib/python3.13t`, see python/cpython#121103:

```
❯ ls ~/mc/envs/bug/lib/python3.13t/site-packages
README.txt           pip                  pip-24.1.2.dist-info

❯ ls ~/mc/envs/bug/lib/python3.13/site-packages
__pycache__               imagesize-1.3.0.dist-info imagesize.py
```

### What happened?

All Python packages in an environment containing the experimental free-threading variant of Python 3.13 are reported as coming from the `pypi` channel. This behavior does not occur when using the GIL variant of Python 3.13.

My guess would be that the logic responsible for channel assignment does not take into account that the site-packages directory contains a `t` suffix after the version, `lib/python3.13t/site-packages`.

### What happened?

Installing `noarch: python` packages in an environment with a free-threading version of Python 3.13 results in the package contents not being available for import. 

```
❯ conda create -n bug -c ad-testing/label/py313_nogil python=3.13.0b4 pip --yes

The issue here is that the `noarch: python` package is installed into `<PREFIX>/lib/python3.13/site-packages` but the free-threading libraries are expected in `<PREFIX>/lib/python3.13t`, see python/cpython#121103:

```
❯ ls ~/mc/envs/bug/lib/python3.13t/site-packages
README.txt           pip                  pip-24.1.2.dist-info

❯ ls ~/mc/envs/bug/lib/python3.13/site-packages
__pycache__               imagesize-1.3.0.dist-info imagesize.py
```

# Problem description

Briefly the issue is that the experimental free-threading build of Python 3.13 on POSIX systems (Linux, macOS) includes a `t` suffix in the directory where Python libraries are stored. This changes the location of the site-packages directory from `<prefix>/lib/python3.13/site-packages` to `<prefix>/lib/python3.13t/site-packages`. 

When installing noarch: python packages, conda copies files into the non-suffixed site-packages directory. This  results in modules in these packages not being able to be importable unless the `python` package provides some form of accommodation to allow modules to be loaded from the non-suffixed site-packages directory.


# Patching CPython
One option to fix this behavior is to revert the change in CPython that adds the `t` suffix. A free-threading `python` package with this change would work identically to a GIL build of CPython. Noarch: python packages could be installed and imported without needing any accommodation. This would also address the bug reported in #14153.

The downside of this approach is that this changes the upstream project. The `t` suffix may be considered a feature of the free-threading build rather than something that distributors can exclude. 

Additionally, this change would need to be applied to all free-threading builds of the `python` package. Any package that did not apply this patch or packages built using a non-patched `python` would be incompatible with those with the patch. Essentially, this change runs the risk of creating two sets of incompatible free-threading packages, those created with the patch and those without.


# Fixing conda 
If CPython is not changed, then conda should be fixed to install the files from `noarch: python` packages into the correct location. There were a number of suggestions on how this could be accomplished:

* Query the ABI tag or location of site-packages after `python` is installed into the environment. One downside of this approach is that it will not work when creating non-native environments where the interpreter cannot be run.
* Examine what site-packages directories exist on the filesystem after the `python` package is installed and select an appropriate one. Conda-build does something along these lines to set the `SP_DIR` environment variable.
* Use information from the build string of the `python` or `python_abi` packages to determine the site-packages directory. This has the benefit of not requiring the `python` package to be installed prior to other packages.

# Accommodating existing conda versions 
The free-threading builds of the `python` package may wish to include some accommodation for installing noarch: python packages with conda which does not include the above fix. 

Two methods that would work:
* Add a symlink from the non-suffixed directory to the suffixed directory.
* Add the non-suffixed directory to sys.path at interpreter startup using sitecustomize.py (a .pth file could also be used to accomplish this addition).

# What's next?
What are the next steps here? 
Is further discussion on these options needed? 
Should a proposal be made? 
Is a short CEP in order?

# Proposal
If a concrete proposal would be helpful my personal preference would be:

* Do not modify CPython to change the site-packages directory. Free-threading builds will include the t suffix.
* Update conda to take into consideration the python_abi package build when determining where noarch: python packages should be installed.
* Free-threading python package should add the non-suffixed directory to sys.path when it exists using sitecustomize.py or a .pth file.

Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open reports and couldn't find a duplicate

### What happened?

Because of the [regex used by conda-build](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/blob/24.7.1/conda_build/metadata.py#L252-L261) selectors will be detected in locations where users would likely not expect them to be.

Some examples:

``` yaml
package:
  name: example
  version: 1.0.0
test:
  commands:
    - echo "An example of slicing an array is arr[1:None]
      neat right!"
```
(combining these last two lines or adding a comma results in a working recipe)

and 
``` yaml
package:
  name: example
  version: 1.0.0
test:
  commands:
    - echo "I told #user to slice the array with array[1:None] which worked"
```

For clarity a selector will be detected on any line where 
* There are a pair of brackets `[...]` at the end of the line (after removing whitespace).
* There are a pair of brackets `[...]` after a comment `#`.

I suspect this issue has been in conda-build for so long that is cannot be nicely addressed without breaking backwards compatibility. I would be happy to see this issue marked as wont-fix.

Similar issues have been reported in the past but are now closed. For example #4654.

```

Note that these tests pass with clang 16 or above (at least for all the version I've tested) as well as clang 15.0.0 provided by Apple via XCode.

An environment can be created with a toolchain which shows this behavior using:
```
conda create -n dev -c conda-forge python=3.12 pip clang_osx-arm64=15 clangxx_osx-arm64=15
```

These are the same tests failures from #22933 which were addressed in #22954.

Bisection found that #25781 reintroduced these tests failures, likely because the mitigation in #22954 are not present in highway.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
np.test(verbose=True)
```

### What happened?

Installing `noarch: python` packages in an environment with a free-threading version of Python 3.13 results in the package contents not being available for import. 

```
❯ conda create -n bug -c ad-testing/label/py313_nogil python=3.13.0b4 pip --yes
Channels:
 - ad-testing/label/py313_nogil
 - defaults
Platform: osx-arm64
Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: /Users/jhelmus/mc/envs/bug

  added / updated specs:
    - pip
    - python=3.13.0b4


The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

  bzip2              pkgs/main/osx-arm64::bzip2-1.0.8-h80987f9_6
  ca-certificates    pkgs/main/osx-arm64::ca-certificates-2024.7.2-hca03da5_0
  expat              pkgs/main/osx-arm64::expat-2.6.2-h313beb8_0
  libcxx             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::libcxx-14.0.6-h848a8c0_0
  libffi             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::libffi-3.4.4-hca03da5_1
  ncurses            pkgs/main/osx-arm64::ncurses-6.4-h313beb8_0
  openssl            pkgs/main/osx-arm64::openssl-3.0.14-h80987f9_0
  pip                ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::pip-24.1.2-py313_0
  python             ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::python-3.13.0b4-h99e199e_nogil_0
  python_abi         ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::python_abi-3.13-4_cp313t
  readline           pkgs/main/osx-arm64::readline-8.2-h1a28f6b_0
  sqlite             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::sqlite-3.45.3-h80987f9_0
  tk                 pkgs/main/osx-arm64::tk-8.6.14-h6ba3021_0
  tzdata             pkgs/main/noarch::tzdata-2024a-h04d1e81_0
  xz                 pkgs/main/osx-arm64::xz-5.4.6-h80987f9_1
  zlib               pkgs/main/osx-arm64::zlib-1.2.13-h18a0788_1



Downloading and Extracting Packages:

Preparing transaction: done
Verifying transaction: done
Executing transaction: done
#
# To activate this environment, use
#
#     $ conda activate bug
#
# To deactivate an active environment, use
#
#     $ conda deactivate


❯ conda activate bug
❯ python --version
Python 3.13.0b4
❯ python -c "import pip; print(pip.__version__)"
24.1.2

❯ conda install -c defaults -c conda-forge imagesize --yes
Channels:
 - defaults
 - conda-forge
 - ad-testing/label/py313_nogil
Platform: osx-arm64
Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: /Users/jhelmus/mc/envs/bug

  added / updated specs:
    - imagesize


The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

  imagesize          pkgs/main/noarch::imagesize-1.3.0-pyhd3eb1b0_0



Downloading and Extracting Packages:

Preparing transaction: done
Verifying transaction: done
Executing transaction: done

❯ python -c "import imagesize"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
    import imagesize
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'imagesize'

```

The issue here is that the `noarch: python` package is installed into `<PREFIX>/lib/python3.13/site-packages` but the free-threading libraries are expected in `<PREFIX>/lib/python3.13t`, see python/cpython#121103:

```
❯ ls ~/mc/envs/bug/lib/python3.13t/site-packages
README.txt           pip                  pip-24.1.2.dist-info

❯ ls ~/mc/envs/bug/lib/python3.13/site-packages
__pycache__               imagesize-1.3.0.dist-info imagesize.py
```

Changes: * Update to version 24.1.2 * Bump minimum Python version to 3.8. Python 3.7 support was removed in version 24.1. * Use the pip source to install itself. Note that setup.py was removed in version 24.1. * Remove the run requirements on `setuptools` and `wheel`. They are only needed to build the package not at runtime. ### Issue

pip 24.1.2 was released on 2024-07-31 07:20:55. Ever since it was released and our build system started to pick it up we have a build error that says:

```
error: invalid command 'bdist_wheel'
```

Splitting `pip` into `pip-core` and `pip` creates two transitions. 
The first would is packages to using `pip-core` to run `pip check` in tests or when they use non-setuptools backends. This would not be required but it will crop up.

The second is when `pip-core` is removed. At that point all the feedstocks that made the change above need to switch back.

IMHO adding `pip-core` creates future work and does not offer people who want to follow best practices (declaring their full dependencies) a straight forward  path.  I'd rather see: 
* feedstocks update their dependencies 
*  re-add setuptools and wheel as pip run requirements for the time being and set up a linter to flag packages that only depend on `pip`. After some amount of time (6 months?) drop the dependencies on setuptools and wheel.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pip-feedstock/issues/comments/2261031917/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

pip 24.1.2 was released on 2024-07-31 07:20:55. Ever since it was released and our build system started to pick it up we have a build error that says:

```
error: invalid command 'bdist_wheel'
```

After doing a diff on the dependencies installed I noticed the following:

```
26c26
<     pip:               24.0-pyhd8ed1ab_0          conda-forge
---
>     pip:               24.1.2-pyhd8ed1ab_0        conda-forge
38d37
<     wheel:             0.43.0-pyhd8ed1ab_1        conda-forge
```

it seems like going from version 24.0 to 24.1.2 no longer installs the wheel package as a dependency, which seems to be the source of the problem.

> 
> 1. readd wheel as a dependency of `pip` as a temporary lie
> 2. update the bulk of the most important feedstocks to explicitly declare `wheel` explicitly
> 3. release a build 1 of `pip` that drops the `wheel` dependency and clean up the remaining breakage
> 
> Introducing multiple `pip` packages seems really confusing, like more bloat for the knowledge base.

I like this approach, but you need to add both `wheel` and `setuptools` or just `setuptools >= 70.1.0` (wheel is vendored in setuptools [starting in 70.1.0](https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/stable/history.html#v70-1-0)).  `setuptools` is the build backend which many feedstocks will call when running `python -m pip install ....`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pip-feedstock/issues/comments/2261091498/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

pip 24.1.2 was released on 2024-07-31 07:20:55. Ever since it was released and our build system started to pick it up we have a build error that says:

```
error: invalid command 'bdist_wheel'
```

After doing a diff on the dependencies installed I noticed the following:

```
26c26
<     pip:               24.0-pyhd8ed1ab_0          conda-forge
---
>     pip:               24.1.2-pyhd8ed1ab_0        conda-forge
38d37
<     wheel:             0.43.0-pyhd8ed1ab_1        conda-forge
```

it seems like going from version 24.0 to 24.1.2 no longer installs the wheel package as a dependency, which seems to be the source of the problem.

Solution to issue cannot be found in the documentation.

- [X] I checked the documentation.

### Issue

pip 24.1.2 was released on 2024-07-31 07:20:55. Ever since it was released and our build system started to pick it up we have a build error that says:

```
error: invalid command 'bdist_wheel'
```

After doing a diff on the dependencies installed I noticed the following:

```
26c26
<     pip:               24.0-pyhd8ed1ab_0          conda-forge
---
>     pip:               24.1.2-pyhd8ed1ab_0        conda-forge
38d37
<     wheel:             0.43.0-pyhd8ed1ab_1        conda-forge
```

it seems like going from version 24.0 to 24.1.2 no longer installs the wheel package as a dependency, which seems to be the source of the problem.

### What happened?

Installing `noarch: python` packages in an environment with a free-threading version of Python 3.13 results in the package contents not being available for import. 

```
❯ conda create -n bug -c ad-testing/label/py313_nogil python=3.13.0b4 pip --yes
Channels:
 - ad-testing/label/py313_nogil
 - defaults
Platform: osx-arm64
Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: /Users/jhelmus/mc/envs/bug

  added / updated specs:
    - pip
    - python=3.13.0b4


The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

  bzip2              pkgs/main/osx-arm64::bzip2-1.0.8-h80987f9_6
  ca-certificates    pkgs/main/osx-arm64::ca-certificates-2024.7.2-hca03da5_0
  expat              pkgs/main/osx-arm64::expat-2.6.2-h313beb8_0
  libcxx             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::libcxx-14.0.6-h848a8c0_0
  libffi             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::libffi-3.4.4-hca03da5_1
  ncurses            pkgs/main/osx-arm64::ncurses-6.4-h313beb8_0
  openssl            pkgs/main/osx-arm64::openssl-3.0.14-h80987f9_0
  pip                ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::pip-24.1.2-py313_0
  python             ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::python-3.13.0b4-h99e199e_nogil_0
  python_abi         ad-testing/label/py313_nogil/osx-arm64::python_abi-3.13-4_cp313t
  readline           pkgs/main/osx-arm64::readline-8.2-h1a28f6b_0
  sqlite             pkgs/main/osx-arm64::sqlite-3.45.3-h80987f9_0
  tk                 pkgs/main/osx-arm64::tk-8.6.14-h6ba3021_0
  tzdata             pkgs/main/noarch::tzdata-2024a-h04d1e81_0
  xz                 pkgs/main/osx-arm64::xz-5.4.6-h80987f9_1
  zlib               pkgs/main/osx-arm64::zlib-1.2.13-h18a0788_1



Downloading and Extracting Packages:

Preparing transaction: done
Verifying transaction: done
Executing transaction: done
#
# To activate this environment, use
#
#     $ conda activate bug
#
# To deactivate an active environment, use
#
#     $ conda deactivate


❯ conda activate bug
❯ python --version
Python 3.13.0b4
❯ python -c "import pip; print(pip.__version__)"
24.1.2

❯ conda install -c defaults -c conda-forge imagesize --yes
Channels:
 - defaults
 - conda-forge
 - ad-testing/label/py313_nogil
Platform: osx-arm64
Collecting package metadata (repodata.json): done
Solving environment: done

  environment location: /Users/jhelmus/mc/envs/bug

  added / updated specs:
    - imagesize

  imagesize          pkgs/main/noarch::imagesize-1.3.0-pyhd3eb1b0_0



Downloading and Extracting Packages:

Preparing transaction: done
Verifying transaction: done
Executing transaction: done

❯ python -c "import imagesize"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
    import imagesize
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'imagesize'

```

The issue here is that the `noarch: python` package is installed into `<PREFIX>/lib/python3.13/site-packages` but the free-threading libraries are expected in `<PREFIX>/lib/python3.13t`, see python/cpython#121103:

```
❯ ls ~/mc/envs/bug/lib/python3.13t/site-packages
README.txt           pip                  pip-24.1.2.dist-info

❯ ls ~/mc/envs/bug/lib/python3.13/site-packages
__pycache__               imagesize-1.3.0.dist-info imagesize.py
```

Comment:

Following the instructions for use of this package in the [knowledge base](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base/#microarch) results in packages that also depends on the microarchitecture level of the host machine if the recipe includes a requirements/host section. 

For example:

meta.yaml
``` yaml
package:
  name: sample
  version: "1.0.0"


requirements:
  build:
    - x86_64-microarch-level {{ microarch_level }}  # [unix and x86_64]
    - {{ compiler('c') }}
  host:
    - ca-certificates
```

conda_build_config.yaml
``` yaml
microarch_level:  # [unix and x86_64]
  - 1  # [unix and x86_64]
  - 3  # [unix and x86_64]
  - 4  # [unix and x86_64]
```

Building this on host with a Zen4 cpu:

``` 
# conda info | grep archspec
       virtual packages : __archspec=1=zen4
# conda build .
...
# conda search --info -c local sample --override-channels
Loading channels: done
sample 1.0.0 h00d5617_0
-----------------------
file name   : sample-1.0.0-h00d5617_0.tar.bz2
name        : sample
version     : 1.0.0
build       : h00d5617_0
build number: 0
size        : 3 KB
subdir      : linux-64
url         : file:///opt/conda/conda-bld/linux-64/sample-1.0.0-h00d5617_0.tar.bz2
md5         : c034688ab7ce82943fe6ecabac19d488
timestamp   : 2024-06-04 21:12:10 UTC
dependencies:
  - _x86_64-microarch-level >=3
  - _x86_64-microarch-level >=4
  - libgcc-ng >=13


sample 1.0.0 h9becff6_0
-----------------------
file name   : sample-1.0.0-h9becff6_0.tar.bz2
name        : sample
version     : 1.0.0
build       : h9becff6_0
build number: 0
size        : 3 KB
subdir      : linux-64
url         : file:///opt/conda/conda-bld/linux-64/sample-1.0.0-h9becff6_0.tar.bz2
md5         : 66bdae327a171000c4a169c3f78bbbcb
timestamp   : 2024-06-04 21:12:20 UTC
dependencies:
  - _x86_64-microarch-level >=1
  - _x86_64-microarch-level >=4
  - libgcc-ng >=13


sample 1.0.0 hafcd524_0
-----------------------
file name   : sample-1.0.0-hafcd524_0.tar.bz2
name        : sample
version     : 1.0.0
build       : hafcd524_0
build number: 0
size        : 3 KB
subdir      : linux-64
url         : file:///opt/conda/conda-bld/linux-64/sample-1.0.0-hafcd524_0.tar.bz2
md5         : 697ddd2a00308ebf314400beb6484876
timestamp   : 2024-06-04 21:12:15 UTC
dependencies:
  - _x86_64-microarch-level >=4
  - libgcc-ng >=13
```

Notice that all of the packages have a dependency on the host microarch-level (>=4). This is a result of the strong run_export in the `_x86_64-microarch-level` package being applied from the `host` section after it is added as a requirement by the strong run_export from the `x86_64-microarch-level` package in the `build` section. Strong run_exports from the `build` section [do apply transitively](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/blob/24.5.1/conda_build/render.py#L488-L489) in the `host` section (or at least have for the last ~7 years). 

Rendering microarch_level 1 gives a good look at what is happening:

```
e# conda render .
--------------
Hash contents:
--------------
{'c_compiler': 'gcc', 'microarch_level': '1', 'target_platform': 'linux-64'}
----------
meta.yaml:
----------
package:
  name: sample
  version: 1.0.0
build: {}
requirements:
  build:
    - libsanitizer 13.2.0 h6ddb7a1_7
    - gcc_impl_linux-64 13.2.0 h9eb54c0_7
    - gcc_linux-64 13.2.0 h1ed452b_3
    - libgcc-ng 13.2.0 h77fa898_7
    - x86_64-microarch-level 1 1_k10
    - ld_impl_linux-64 2.40 hf3520f5_2
    - sysroot_linux-64 2.12 he073ed8_17
    - libstdcxx-ng 13.2.0 hc0a3c3a_7
    - libgomp 13.2.0 h77fa898_7
    - binutils_linux-64 2.40 hdade7a5_3
    - binutils_impl_linux-64 2.40 ha1999f0_2
    - _openmp_mutex 4.5 2_gnu
    - _libgcc_mutex 0.1 conda_forge
    - kernel-headers_linux-64 2.6.32 he073ed8_17
    - libgcc-devel_linux-64 13.2.0 hceb6213_107
  host:
    - _x86_64-microarch-level 4 1_zen4
    - ca-certificates 2024.6.2 hbcca054_0
    - _libgcc_mutex 0.1 conda_forge
    - libgomp 13.2.0 h77fa898_7
    - _openmp_mutex 4.5 2_gnu
    - libgcc-ng 13.2.0 h77fa898_7
  run:
    - _x86_64-microarch-level >=1
    - _x86_64-microarch-level >=4
    - libgcc-ng >=13
about: {}
extra:
  copy_test_source_files: true
  final: true
```

First the strong run_export on `_x86_64-microarch-level >=1` is added to the `host` and `run` section from `x86_64-microarch-level 1` in the `build` section. 

Then the `host` section is evaluated. The solver selects the _x86_64-microarch-level=4=1_zen4` package and applies the `_x86_64-microarch-level >=4` strong run_export to the `run` section.

I see a few option:
* This is bug in conda-build, transitive run_exports should not propagate from the build section to the host section. That said this bug has existed for considerable time and may be a "feature" as this point.
* Remove the all run_exports from the `_x86_64-microarch-level` package. Rely on the `x86_64-microarch-level` to add them to the `run` section.
* Introduce a new package with no run_exports that the `x86_64-microarch-level` package specifies as a strong run_export. This package would have a run dependency on the appropriate `_x86_64-microarch-level` package.

cf #5 which touches on some of these points.

requirements","user":{"login":"jjhelmus","id":1050278,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTAyNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1050278?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus","html_url":"https://github.com/jjhelmus","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6182023744,"node_id":"LA_kwDOKqoLMM8AAAABcHoyQA","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/microarch-level-feedstock/labels/question","name":"question","color":"d876e3","default":true,"description":"Further information is requested"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-06-04T21:29:56Z","updated_at":"2024-06-05T13:53:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Comment:

Following the instructions for use of this package in the [knowledge base](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base/#microarch) results in packages that also depends on the microarchitecture level of the host machine if the recipe includes a requirements/host section. 

For example:

meta.yaml
``` yaml
package:
  name: sample
  version: "1.0.0"


requirements:
  build:
    - x86_64-microarch-level {{ microarch_level }}  # [unix and x86_64]
    - {{ compiler('c') }}
  host:
    - ca-certificates
```

conda_build_config.yaml
``` yaml
microarch_level:  # [unix and x86_64]
  - 1  # [unix and x86_64]
  - 3  # [unix and x86_64]
  - 4  # [unix and x86_64]
```

Building this on host with a Zen4 cpu:

``` 
# conda info | grep archspec
       virtual packages : __archspec=1=zen4
# conda build .
...
# conda search --info -c local sample --override-channels
Loading channels: done
sample 1.0.0 h00d5617_0
-----------------------
file name   : sample-1.0.0-h00d5617_0.tar.bz2
name        : sample
version     : 1.0.0
build       : h00d5617_0
build number: 0
size        : 3 KB
subdir      : linux-64
url         : file:///opt/conda/conda-bld/linux-64/sample-1.0.0-h00d5617_0.tar.bz2
md5         : c034688ab7ce82943fe6ecabac19d488
timestamp   : 2024-06-04 21:12:10 UTC
dependencies:
  - _x86_64-microarch-level >=3
  - _x86_64-microarch-level >=4
  - libgcc-ng >=13


sample 1.0.0 h9becff6_0
-----------------------
file name   : sample-1.0.0-h9becff6_0.tar.bz2
name        : sample
version     : 1.0.0
build       : h9becff6_0
build number: 0
size        : 3 KB
subdir      : linux-64
url         : file:///opt/conda/conda-bld/linux-64/sample-1.0.0-h9becff6_0.tar.bz2
md5         : 66bdae327a171000c4a169c3f78bbbcb
timestamp   : 2024-06-04 21:12:20 UTC
dependencies:
  - _x86_64-microarch-level >=1
  - _x86_64-microarch-level >=4
  - libgcc-ng >=13


sample 1.0.0 hafcd524_0
-----------------------
file name   : sample-1.0.0-hafcd524_0.tar.bz2
name        : sample
version     : 1.0.0
build       : hafcd524_0
build number: 0
size        : 3 KB
subdir      : linux-64
url         : file:///opt/conda/conda-bld/linux-64/sample-1.0.0-hafcd524_0.tar.bz2
md5         : 697ddd2a00308ebf314400beb6484876
timestamp   : 2024-06-04 21:12:15 UTC
dependencies:
  - _x86_64-microarch-level >=4
  - libgcc-ng >=13
```

Notice that all of the packages have a dependency on the host microarch-level (>=4). This is a result of the strong run_export in the `_x86_64-microarch-level` package being applied from the `host` section after it is added as a requirement by the strong run_export from the `x86_64-microarch-level` package in the `build` section. Strong run_exports from the `build` section [do apply transitively](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/blob/24.5.1/conda_build/render.py#L488-L489) in the `host` section (or at least have for the last ~7 years). 

Rendering microarch_level 1 gives a good look at what is happening:

```
e# conda render .
--------------
Hash contents:
--------------
{'c_compiler': 'gcc', 'microarch_level': '1', 'target_platform': 'linux-64'}
----------
meta.yaml:
----------
package:
  name: sample
  version: 1.0.0
build: {}
requirements:
  build:
    - libsanitizer 13.2.0 h6ddb7a1_7
    - gcc_impl_linux-64 13.2.0 h9eb54c0_7
    - gcc_linux-64 13.2.0 h1ed452b_3
    - libgcc-ng 13.2.0 h77fa898_7
    - x86_64-microarch-level 1 1_k10
    - ld_impl_linux-64 2.40 hf3520f5_2
    - sysroot_linux-64 2.12 he073ed8_17
    - libstdcxx-ng 13.2.0 hc0a3c3a_7
    - libgomp 13.2.0 h77fa898_7
    - binutils_linux-64 2.40 hdade7a5_3
    - binutils_impl_linux-64 2.40 ha1999f0_2
    - _openmp_mutex 4.5 2_gnu
    - _libgcc_mutex 0.1 conda_forge
    - kernel-headers_linux-64 2.6.32 he073ed8_17
    - libgcc-devel_linux-64 13.2.0 hceb6213_107
  host:
    - _x86_64-microarch-level 4 1_zen4
    - ca-certificates 2024.6.2 hbcca054_0
    - _libgcc_mutex 0.1 conda_forge
    - libgomp 13.2.0 h77fa898_7
    - _openmp_mutex 4.5 2_gnu
    - libgcc-ng 13.2.0 h77fa898_7
  run:
    - _x86_64-microarch-level >=1
    - _x86_64-microarch-level >=4
    - libgcc-ng >=13
about: {}
extra:
  copy_test_source_files: true
  final: true
```

First the strong run_export on `_x86_64-microarch-level >=1` is added to the `host` and `run` section from `x86_64-microarch-level 1` in the `build` section. 

Then the `host` section is evaluated. The solver selects the `_x86_64-microarch-level=4=1_zen4` package and applies the `_x86_64-microarch-level >=4` strong run_export to the `run` section.

I see a few option:
* This is bug in conda-build, transitive run_exports should not propagate from the build section to the host section. That said this bug has existed for considerable time and may be a "feature" as this point.
* Remove the all run_exports from the `_x86_64-microarch-level` package. Rely on the `x86_64-microarch-level` to add them to the `run` section.
* Introduce a new package with no run_exports that the `x86_64-microarch-level` package specifies as a strong run_export. This package would have a run dependency on the appropriate `_x86_64-microarch-level` package.

cf #5 which touches on some of these points.

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open requests and couldn't find a duplicate

### What is the idea?

To facilitate the review of incoming Adobe Sign CLA signatures, we'll need to create a "CLA Reviewers" subteam per governance policy.

### Why is this needed?

@conda/steering-council Opinions on this proposal?

### Proposal

I'm proposing to form a new "CLA Reviewers" subteam to help with https://github.com/conda/infrastructure/issues/932

### Team Charter

Following the governance policy, this is a [static](https://github.com/conda-incubator/governance/tree/963a16ee02126229ec57614b000e3480d8cea26d#static-charter) charter with the condition that the new CLA Reviewers team only consists of steering council members for compliance reasons.

The "CLA Reviewers" subteam helps with reviewing, facilitating feedback and approving (or denying) incoming CLA documents, through a public pull request based process. 

The CLA documents will be accessible to the team via a NumFOCUS provided Google Drive or similar.

This new team will be responsible for taking on the work that the "Infrastructure" team has done while reviewing similar signed documents of the previous Anaconda Inc-specific CLA.

To reduce the risk of bottlenecks in processing new CLA documents, the team must have at least 3 active members, and automatically fall back to the whole conda steering council if that number falls below it.

#### Work areas

- review incoming signed CLA documents via Adobe sign
- approving PRs in the repository where new CLA requests arrive (e.g. `conda/infrastructure` or `conda/governance`) as PR based on the process described in https://github.com/conda/infrastructure/issues/932
- responding to requests by the rest of the conda steering council, NumFOCUS legal etc

#### Scope

The "CLA Reviewer" team works as delegates of the conda steering council and follows the Conda Code of Conduct.

Here are copies of the new NumFOCUS specific CLA (location not final):

- The updated CLA public documentation: https://acrobat.adobe.com/id/urn:aaid:sc:VA6C2:6d77823b-ba54-483f-aa7b-bec5a2f00ab6

- The actual CLA form to sign: https://acrobat.adobe.com/id/urn:aaid:sc:VA6C2:080a6efe-79c5-4934-b4f6-c018b04ea1e3

#### Proposed members:

- @jezdez
- @chenghlee
- @jaimergp

When running `conda env update`, it is possible that the update may change conda itself. If this occurs it may not be possible to import modules that are not in the cache because the underlying files have been removed.  

To account for this, all modules that may be used as part of the update flow need to be imported before updating the environment.

To facilitate this, move the `get_filename` and `print_result` function out of `conda/env/env.py` and into a new module, `conda/env/env_util.py`. With this change there is no need to delay imports to inside the body of the `print_result` function, these imports can be placed at the top of the file.  Since the imports are not delayed, they will be in the module cache when the environment is updated.

fixes #13560

### What happened?

On the macOS of x86-64 the version of the `__osx` virtual package depend on what SDK CPython in the base environment was built with. Specifically it can either be >=10.16,<11.0 if CPython was built with macOS SDK <11 or >=11.0 if built with SDK >=11. This is a result of the 10.x compatibility rules that comes into play when reporting the OS version starting with Big Sur. For example see [Big Sur is both 10.16 and 11.0 – it’s official](https://eclecticlight.co/2020/07/21/big-sur-is-both-10-16-and-11-0-its-official/). 

This difference in versions for the `__osx` virtual packages make it difficult for package builders to determine an appropriate constraint to add when they need to describe the version of macOS their package is compatible with. This also introduced a hurdle for end users who may be blocked from installing packages that use have a dependency on `__osx>=11.0`.

As an example of this inconsistency:

``` 
❯ sw_vers
ProductName:		macOS
ProductVersion:		14.4.1
BuildVersion:		23E224

❯ conda create --name test_py39_defaults --platform osx-64 python=3.9 conda --yes
...
❯ ./mc/envs/test_py39_defaults/condabin/conda info | grep __osx
                          __osx=10.16=0
❯ conda create --name test_py39_cf --platform osx-64 python=3.9 conda --override-channels --channel conda-forge --yes
❯ ./mc/envs/test_py39_cf/condabin/conda info | grep __osx
                          __osx=14.4.1=0
```

One potential solution for this issue would be to translate the versions >=10.16,<11.0 into it's >=11.0 equivalent. This could be tricky going forward as future version are not known AFAIK.

In turn this is determined by [reading the version from a plist file](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.9/Lib/platform.py#L411-L430) in `platform._mac_ver_xml`. 

One option would be to this read this file via a subprocess with `SYSTEM_VERSION_COMPAT=0` set:

``` python
def non_compat_mac_ver() -> Optional[str]:
    # adapted from _mac_ver_xml in the stdlib platform module
    fn = '/System/Library/CoreServices/SystemVersion.plist'
    if not os.path.exists(fn):
        if 'SDKROOT' in os.environ:
            fn = os.environ['SDKROOT'] + fn
            if not os.path.exists(fn):
                return None
        else:
            return None
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["/bin/cat", fn],
        capture_output=True,
        env = {"SYSTEM_VERSION_COMPAT": "0"}
    )
    pl = plistlib.loads(result.stdout)
    release = pl['ProductVersion']
    return release
 ```
 
 This returns the non-compatible version:
 ```
 ❯ cat example.py 
 ... 
 import platform
orig_ver = platform.mac_ver()[0]
non_compat_ver = non_compat_mac_ver()
print(f"Original version: {orig_ver}")
print(f"Non-compat version: {non_compat_ver}")

❯ python example.py 
Original version: 10.16
Non-compat version: 14.4.1
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/comments/2072995230/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

``` python
def non_compat_mac_ver() -> Optional[str]:
    result = subprocess.run(
        [
            sys.executable,
            "-c",
            "import platform; print(platform.mac_ver()[0])"
        ],
        env = {"SYSTEM_VERSION_COMPAT": "0"},
        capture_output=True,
        encoding="utf-8",
    )
    return result.stdout
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/comments/2073014294/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open requests and couldn't find a duplicate

### What is the idea?

We recently created a new undocumented, `channel_settings` parameter:

- https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/12239

There are still some things left to do before we make this ready for real use.

### Why is this needed?

_No response_

### What should happen?

(from @mbargull)

- If channel key is not present, either:
  - error or
  - warn and discard the entry (IDK/haven't thought about what's preferred here).

- In case of duplicate entries with the same channel key in the same file:
  - error/warn, and
  - if only warn, decide if first or last entry should be chosen.

- In case of duplicate entries with the same channel key in different files, decide if we want to
  - override settings in higher priority config files, or
  - merge the channel's setting keys.

(the first option is easier to reason about).



### Additional Context

Note that this is not just a different in the Python version between these two tags as both use Python 3.11 (3.11.7 vs 3.11.5). When running `conda env update`, it is possible that the update may change conda itself. If this occurs it may not be possible to import modules that are not in the cache because the underlying files have been removed.  

To account for this, all modules that may be used as part of the update flow need to be imported before updating the environment.

fixes #13560

- [ ] Add a file to the `news` directory ([using the template](../blob/main/news/TEMPLATE)) for the next release's release notes?
- [ ] Add / update necessary tests?
- [x] Add / update outdated documentation?

Description

When running `conda env update`, it is possible that the update may change conda itself. If this occurs it may not be possible to import modules that are not in the cache because the underlying files have been removed.  

To account for this, all modules that may be used as part of the update flow need to be imported before updating the environment.

fixes #13560 

### Checklist - did you ...

- [ ] Add a file to the `news` directory ([using the template](../blob/main/news/TEMPLATE)) for the next release's release notes?
- [ ] Add / update necessary tests?
- [x] Add / update outdated documentation?

{"action":"opened","number":13743,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/pulls/13743","id":1796686335,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAF8kJs5rF0H_","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/13743","diff_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/13743.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/13743.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/13743","number":13743,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"move conda env utils into their own modules.","user":{"login":"jjhelmus","id":1050278,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTAyNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1050278?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus","html_url":"https://github.com/jjhelmus","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"### Description

When running `conda env update`, it is possible that the update may change conda itself. If this occurs it may not be possible to import modules that are not in the cache because the underlying files have been removed.  

To account for this, all modules that may be used as part of the update flow need to be imported before updating the environment.

To facilitate this, move the `get_filename` and `print_result` function out of `conda/env/env.py` and into a new module, `conda/env/env_util.py`. With this change there is no need to delay imports to inside the body of the `print_result` function, these imports can be placed at the top of the file.  Since the imports are not delayed, they will be in the module cache when the environment is updated.

fixes #13560 

### Checklist - did you ...

- [ ] Add a file to the `news` directory ([using the template](../blob/main/news/TEMPLATE)) for the next release's release notes?

Description

When running `conda env update`, it is possible that the update may change conda itself. If this occurs it may not be possible to import modules that are not in the cache because the underlying files have been removed.  

To account for this, all modules that may be used as part of the update flow need to be imported before updating the environment.

To facilitate this, move the `get_filename` and `print_result` function out of `conda/env/env.py` and into a new module, `conda/env/env_util.py`. With this change there is no need to delay imports to inside the body of the `print_result` function, these imports can be placed at the top of the file.  Since the imports are not delayed, they will be in the module cache when the environment is updated.

fixes #13560 

### Checklist - did you ...

- [ ] Add a file to the `news` directory ([using the template](../blob/main/news/TEMPLATE)) for the next release's release notes?
- [ ] Add / update necessary tests?

Description

When running `conda env update`, it is possible that the update may change conda itself. If this occurs it may not be possible to import modules that are not in the cache because the underlying files have been removed.  

To account for this, all modules that may be used as part of the update flow need to be imported before updating the environment.

fixes #13560 

### Checklist - did you ...

- [ ] Add a file to the `news` directory ([using the template](../blob/main/news/TEMPLATE)) for the next release's release notes?
- [ ] Add / update necessary tests?
- [x] Add / update outdated documentation?

I wrote a test that removes conda modules from `sys.modules`, monkey patches `conda.env.installers.conda.install` and records the number of modules in `sys.modules` and then performs an update and compares the number of modules in `sys.modules`. In isolation this test can detected the import of new conda modules in `print_results` but when run as part of a test suite it produced new errors, removing items from `sys.modules` does not seem to be a safe option given how plugins are loaded.

I don't see how the original bug can be reproduced in a test as it changes the Python version in the environment when conda itself is running.

Any suggestions would be helpful.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/issues/comments/2026150678/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Hello! Thanks for submitting a PR! To help make things go a bit more
     smoothly, we would appreciate it if you follow this template. -->

### Description

This allows an [FSSpec](https://filesystem-spec.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) URI to be provided as a `remote_definition` to `conda env create`.

This allows reading data using any of the [built-in protocols](https://filesystem-spec.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#built-in-implementations) or from a growing list of [3rd party packages](https://filesystem-spec.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#other-known-implementations) that can be installed.

If the remote definition is not of the form `protocol://path` it will attempt to find it using the BinstarSpec.

Here are some examples using built-in drivers:

```text
conda env create https://github.com/AlbertDeFusco/windturbines/raw/main/environment.yml
conda env create github://albertdefusco:windturbines@main/environment.yml
```

Hello! Thanks for submitting a PR! To help make things go a bit more
     smoothly, we would appreciate it if you follow this template. -->

### Description

This allows an [FSSpec](https://filesystem-spec.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) URI to be provided as a `remote_definition` to `conda env create`.

This allows reading data using any of the [built-in protocols](https://filesystem-spec.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#built-in-implementations) or from a growing list of [3rd party packages](https://filesystem-spec.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#other-known-implementations) that can be installed.

If the remote definition is not of the form `protocol://path` it will attempt to find it using the BinstarSpec.

Here are some examples using built-in drivers:

```text
conda env create https://github.com/AlbertDeFusco/windturbines/raw/main/environment.yml
conda env create github://albertdefusco:windturbines@main/environment.yml
```

Checklist

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open reports and couldn't find a duplicate

### What happened?

If `sys.exit()` is used in a recipe's `run_test.py` script any import tests in the recipe are skipped.  

This is a result of the import tests being appended to the `run_test.py` script. 

A minimum reproducer of this behavior:

`meta.yaml`
``` yaml
package:
  name: sample
  version: "1.0.0"

requirements:
  run:
    - python >=3.8

test:
  imports:
   - math
   - this_should_fail
```

`run_test.py`
``` python
import sys

def do_test_pass():
    return True

if do_test_pass():
    sys.exit(0)
else:
    sys.exit(1)
```

Description

* Add support for FreeBSD platform and the freebsd-64 subdir.
* Update some non-POSIX uses of `source` and a patch argument which are not supported on FreeBSD.
* Document the new `freebsd` selector and clarify that `unix` includes the FreeBSD platform.
* Use bash on FreeBSD rather than the default shell given the numerous uses of bashisms in recipes.

### Checklist - did you ...

- [x] Add a file to the `news` directory ([using the template](../blob/main/news/TEMPLATE)) for the next release's release notes?
- [ ] Add / update necessary tests?
- [x] Add / update outdated documentation?

Description

* Add support for FreeBSD platform and the freebsd-64 subdir.
* Update some non-POSIX uses of `source` and a patch argument which are not supported on FreeBSD.
* Document the new `freebsd` selector and clarify that `unix` includes the FreeBSD platform.
* Use bash on FreeBSD rather than the default shell given the numerous uses of bashisms in recipes.

### Checklist - did you ...

- [x] Add a file to the `news` directory ([using the template](../blob/main/news/TEMPLATE)) for the next release's release notes?
- [ ] Add / update necessary tests?
- [x] Add / update outdated documentation?

Populate the list of supported platforms tags for the macOS platform using the `mac_platforms` function in packaging.tags.  This given a complete listing of platform tags which is also used by pip.  

This avoiding the need to periodically update a static listing of tags.  The new `MACOS_VERSION` variable will need to be updated periodically to reflect the latest version of macOS.

Likely fixes #403 and # This lock file was generated by conda-lock (https://github.com/conda/conda-lock). DO NOT EDIT!

<!-- Hello! Thanks for submitting a PR! To help make things go a bit more
     smoothly, we would appreciate it if you follow this template. -->

### Description

Add support for the FreeBSD operating system and register `freebsd-64` as a known subdirectory for FreeBSD on x86-64.

As discussed in the [docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.platform), on FreeBSD and other Unix systems`sys.platform` includes the major version number, so `freebsd13` rather than `freebsd`.  Only `freebsd13` is supported in this change. Other major versions can be added to the _platform_map or handled in another manner.

<!-- Hello! Thanks for submitting a PR! To help make things go a bit more
     smoothly, we would appreciate it if you follow this template. -->

### Description

Add support for the FreeBSD operating system and register `freebsd-64` as a known subdirectory for FreeBSD on x86-64.

As discussed in the [docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.platform), on FreeBSD and other Unix systems`sys.platform` includes the major version number, so `freebsd13` rather than `freebsd`.  Only `freebsd13` is supported in this change. Other major versions can be added to the _platform_map or handled in another manner.

* Update some non-POSIX uses of `source` and a patch argument which are not supported on FreeBSD.
* Document the new `freebsd` selector and clarify that `unix` includes the FreeBSD platform.
* Use bash on FreeBSD rather than the default shell given the numerous uses of bashisms in recipes.

- [ ] Add / update necessary tests?
- [x] Add / update outdated documentation?

{"action":"opened","number":4873,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/pulls/4873","id":1340433007,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAPRE585P5WJv","html_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/4873","diff_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/4873.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/4873.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda-build/issues/4873","number":4873,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"check return code when selecting patch type","user":{"login":"jjhelmus","id":1050278,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwNTAyNzg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1050278?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus","html_url":"https://github.com/jjhelmus","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jjhelmus/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"### Description

When selecting between the .native, .lf, and .crlf patches, do not mark the result as successful if a non-zero (failure) return value was returned.

- [X] I added a descriptive title
- [X] I searched open requests and couldn't find a duplicate

### What is the idea?

Currently a package match specification can include the md5 of the package to fully specify the package, for example `python[md5=bd5ef8b4531bcd58ac2c00393a7d8c3f]`.  It would be nice if the sha256 hash could be supplied instead.

### Why is this needed?

This would be beneficial as md5 is no longer considered a safe hash function and replacing it with another hash is often recommended. 

### What should happen?

_No response_

<!-- Hello! Thanks for submitting a PR! To help make things go a bit more
     smoothly, we would appreciate it if you follow this template. -->

### Description

Add support for the FreeBSD operating system and register `freebsd-64` as a known subdirectory for FreeBSD on x86-64.

As discussed in the [docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.platform), on FreeBSD and other Unix systems`sys.platform` includes the major version number, so `freebsd13` rather than `freebsd`.  Only `freebsd13` is supported in this change. Other major versions can be added to the _platform_map or handled in another manner.

This PR is for demonstration and discussion, it is unlikely that these changes should be merged as is.

Add type annotations to the conda_build library created by running the test suite with [MonkeyType](https://monkeytype.readthedocs.io/en/stable/index.html).

This steps used to accomplished this were:
```
make setup
conda activate conda-build
python -m pip install monkeytype
monkeytype run -m pytest
monkeytype list-modules | grep conda_build > modules
cat modules | while read mod
do
    echo "Adding types to $mod"
    monkeytype apply --pep_563 $mod
done
```

### What is the idea?

Type annotations/hints can make code more explicit, easier to understand and by using a static checker can be used to find potential bugs and errors. Support for type annotation has grown considerably in recent released of Python. Many popular library in the Python ecosystem have added type annotation in the last few years.

Adding type annotations to conda-build has a number of benefits including:
- Making the code base more approachable to new contributors.
- Making the API, functions and classes more explicit. 
- Finding bugs and errors by using a static type checker like `mypy`.

Types can be manually added to a code base gradually over time.  Another option is to use a a tool that automatically generates type annotations such as [MonkeyType](https://monkeytype.readthedocs.io/en/stable/index.html) or [PyAnnotate](https://github.com/dropbox/pyannotate). I ran the former to generate the type annotations in #4878.

Disable the user site-packages directory when retrieving the shell code for activation, deactivation and reactivation from CONDA_EXE. The output from these calls are evaluated by the shell and any extraneous output introduced by the user site-packages directory, such as by usercustomize.py or .pth files, will be evaluated.

An alternative would be to use Python in isolated mode (`-I`) to run the CONDA_EXE script. This would also ignore the PYTHON* environment variables and unsafe paths.

Note that this only adds this to Unix style shells.  Windows specific shells (cmd, powershell) may still access the user site-packages directory during activation.

### What happened?

Any text send to stdout during python startup will be executed by `conda activate` and `conda deactivate`.  This behavior can be used to run arbitrary code with content not contained in the conda environment by placing files which effect interpreter startup (`usercustomize.py` or `.pth` files) in the user site-package directory.

For example:
```
❯ conda activate base
❯ python --version
Python 3.10.8
❯ mkdir -p ~/.local/lib/python3.10/site-packages
❯ echo "print('echo This should not be seen')" > ~/.local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/usercustomize.py
❯ conda activate base
This should not be seen
```

This can also be done using a .pth file:

```
❯ rm ~/.local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/usercustomize.py
❯ echo "import sys; print('echo This should not be seen')" > ~/.local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/example.pth
❯ conda activate base
This should not be seen
```

Similar behavior can be caused by creating these files in the standard system site-packages directory but this directory is within the conda environment. 

Related documentation:
* https://docs.python.org/3/library/site.html
* https://docs.python.org/3/using/cmdline.html

Description

Disable the user site-packages directory when retrieving the shell code for activation, deactivation and reactivation from CONDA_EXE. The output from these calls are evaluated by the shell and any extraneous output introduced by the user site-packages directory, such as by usercustomize.py or .pth files, will be evaluated.

An alternative would be to use Python in isolated mode (`-I`) to run the CONDA_EXE script. This would also ignore the PYTHON* environment variables and unsafe paths.

Note that this only adds this to Unix style shells.  Windows specific shells (cmd, powershell) may still access the user site-packages directory during activation.

```
The following packages will be SUPERSEDED by a higher-priority channel:

  anaconda-client    conda-forge/noarch::anaconda-client-1~ --> pkgs/main/linux-64::anaconda-client-1.11.0-py310h06a4308_0 
  beautifulsoup4     conda-forge/noarch::beautifulsoup4-4.~ --> pkgs/main/linux-64::beautifulsoup4-4.11.1-py310h06a4308_0 
  certifi            conda-forge/noarch::certifi-2022.12.7~ --> pkgs/main/linux-64::certifi-2022.12.7-py310h06a4308_0 
  chardet            conda-forge::chardet-5.1.0-py310hff52~ --> pkgs/main::chardet-4.0.0-py310h06a4308_1003 
  conda              conda-forge::conda-23.1.0-py310hff520~ --> pkgs/main::conda-23.1.0-py310h06a4308_0

```  


This occurs in all the Linux conda-forge test jobs.

Since Python 3.7 the Python zipfile module has allowed setting the
default compression level in zipfiles using the `compresslevel`
argument.

Pass the compression level specified on the command line to the ZipFile class.

Description

Since Python 3.7 the Python zipfile module has allowed setting the
default compression level in zipfiles using the `compresslevel`
argument.

Pass the compression level specified on the command line to the ZipFile class.

``` shell
conda create -p ./to_pack python=3.11
conda-pack -p ./to_pack -o test_0.zip --compress-level=0 --force
conda-pack -p ./to_pack -o test_4.zip --compress-level=4 --force
conda-pack -p ./to_pack -o test_9.zip --compress-level=9 --force
conda-pack -p ./to_pack -o test_default.zip --force
```

| level | time (seconds) | size (MB) 
| - | - | - |
| 0 | 1.5 | 163
| 4 | 3.4 | 62
| 9 | 13.3 | 61
default (4) | 3.5 | 62
